title: Before the Inca
bullets: After the collapse of Tiwanaku and Wari around 1000 CE, the central Andes spent four centuries fragmented into smaller polities — the Chimor on the north coast, the Aymara kingdoms around Lake Titicaca, the small Killke chieftaincies in the Cuzco basin.

The Chimor capital of Chan Chan covered roughly 8 square miles of dense adobe construction housing perhaps 60,000 people — almost certainly the largest city in the Americas before Cuzco eventually overtook it.

The Inca's own origin story ran through two parallel myths simultaneously: Manco Capac and Mama Ocllo emerging from Lake Titicaca, and four brothers emerging from caves at Pacaritambo southwest of Cuzco.

Eight semi-legendary sapa incas reigned between Manco Capac and Pachacuti, but few specific events from before 1438 can be reliably dated, and modern scholars treat the early reigns as sitting in the same zone of historicity as the legendary kings of early Rome.

The reigning Sapa Inca Viracocha Inca fled when the Chanka confederation invaded the Cuzco basin around 1438, and his disinherited son Cusi Yupanqui stayed to fight, defeated the Chanka at Yahuar Pampa, took the throne, and renamed himself Pachacuti — 'world-overturner.'

The early Cuzco kingdom inherited the entire Andean institutional toolkit — the ayllu kinship group, reciprocal labor exchange, vertical archipelago, the wak'a religious geography — that had been operating at village level for at least a thousand years.

The reliability cutoff for Inca history is sharp: before 1438, almost everything is uncertain; after 1438, we have detailed Spanish chronicles drawn from quipu records and oral histories from people who had lived under the empire.
subtitle: the small Cuzco kingdom Pachacuti inherited and the Chanka war that opened the throne

title: Pachacuti and the Imperial Burst
bullets: Pachacuti reorganized his expanding state into the Tahuantinsuyu — the Four Regions Together — with Cuzco at the geometric center and four roads radiating outward to mark the boundaries between Chinchaysuyu, Antisuyu, Collasuyu, and Cuntisuyu.

Cuzco was demolished and rebuilt in the shape of a puma, with Sacsayhuaman as the head, the central Huacaypata plaza as the heart, and the junction of the Tullumayo and Saphi rivers as the tail — a city plan, a piece of religious symbolism, and a political statement.

The Coricancha was rebuilt as the empire's most sacred building, with interior walls sheathed in beaten gold and a central courtyard containing a life-sized maize garden of gold and silver dedicated to Inti the sun god.

Sacsayhuaman's three terraced megalithic walls included individual blocks weighing up to 200 tons fitted together so precisely no mortar was needed and a knife blade cannot be inserted between them — at the upper end of pre-modern stone-moving scale.

Machu Picchu was built around 1450 as a royal estate of Pachacuti on a forested ridge 8,000 feet above sea level, abandoned around 1530 before any Spanish soldier reached the highlands, never reported to the conquerors, and rediscovered only in 1911 by Hiram Bingham.

Pachacuti formalized the mit'a labor draft, the Tahuantinsuyu administrative geography, the imperial Inti cult, and the basic template of the road network in roughly thirty-three years — the Inca empire was assembled in a single human lifetime by one human being.

The Inca road system was built on top of the eight-hundred-year-old Wari road network, refurbished and vastly extended — almost every Inca institution turned out to be an inherited Andean precedent scaled up to imperial level.
subtitle: the Chanka war's victor rebuilds Cuzco and forges the Tahuantinsuyu in one reign

title: Topa Inca and Huayna Capac
bullets: Topa Inca Yupanqui completed the conquest of Chimor around 1470 not through battlefield assault but by cutting off the irrigation canals that fed Chan Chan, then resettled hundreds of Chimu master metallurgists at Cuzco where their lost-wax casting transformed Inca metalwork.

Topa Inca's southern campaigns reached the Maule River in central Chile around 1485, where the fierce Mapuche resistance halted further expansion and fixed the empire's southern frontier for the rest of its independent history.

Huayna Capac spent twenty years finishing the conquest of northern Ecuador, ending in around 1515 with the Battle of Yahuarcocha — 'lake of blood' — where thousands of captured Caranqui warriors were executed and thrown into the adjacent lake.

Huayna Capac built a second imperial capital at Tumipampa in modern Cuenca and spent most of the last twenty years of his life there, shifting the empire's center of gravity decisively northward — which mattered enormously when the succession crisis broke out a decade later.

By 1525 the empire covered roughly 770,000 square miles, ran 2,500 miles north to south (longer than the Roman Empire at its greatest extent), governed roughly twelve million people, and operated a road network of 25,000 miles comparable in length to the entire Roman road system.

The Inca built one of the largest empires of their century without writing in the European sense, money, draft animals other than the llama, the wheel, iron tools, or markets — a different solution path to the same problems Persia, Rome, and China had solved with different toolsets.

Almost everything we know about the empire comes from three Spanish chroniclers writing in the decades after the conquest: Cieza de León the soldier-traveler, Juan de Betanzos married into Atahualpa's family, and Inca Garcilaso de la Vega the mestizo son of an Inca princess.
subtitle: the empire reaches its full extent under Pachacuti's son and grandson

title: The Imperial Machine
bullets: The Qhapaq Ñan road network ran around 25,000 miles across two main spines (highland and coastal), crossed several passes above 16,000 feet, and was knitted together by suspension bridges of braided ichu grass that some communities still rebuild annually five centuries later.

The chasqui relay messenger system stationed runners every few miles in pairs at small stone huts; a message could travel 150 miles in a day, roughly matching the Persian royal road and the Mongol yam — achieved on foot at altitude across terrain that would have killed horses.

Tens of thousands of qollqa storehouses arranged in long rows on hillsides above provincial centers held freeze-dried potatoes (chuño), maize, dried llama meat (charqui — the source of the English word 'jerky'), textiles, and weapons — Pizarro's chroniclers reported finding years' worth of food in single provinces.

Mit'a was the universal labor draft requiring every adult male commoner to contribute roughly 60 to 90 days a year to state projects — building roads, maintaining terraces, working mines, serving in the army — and was the engine that built the imperial infrastructure without slavery or wages.

The Inca routinely transplanted entire ethnic groups under the mitma resettlement policy, breaking up potential rebellions and seeding loyal communities in new provinces; over a century of expansion this moved several hundred thousand people across the empire.

Cumbi cloth woven by the aclla 'chosen women' from vicuña wool reached thread counts approaching 250 per inch (modern industrial textiles run 40 to 80 per inch) and served as the standard tribute payment, diplomatic gift, and religious offering of the empire.

The quipu was a knotted-cord recording device handling census, tribute, calendars, and possibly narrative — a base-10 positional system whose numerical aspect is well understood, but whether it encoded language is one of the most contested questions in Andean studies.

The Spanish initially preserved Inca quipucamayocs as a working colonial bureaucracy because they could not run the existing tribute system without them, until the Third Council of Lima ordered the burning of quipus as 'heretical objects' in 1583.
subtitle: how a continent-scale empire ran on knotted cords, runners, and rotational labor

title: Religion, Mummies, and the Sun
bullets: Pachacuti elevated Inti the sun god to chief deity of the imperial state cult, with the Sapa Inca as Inti's living son on earth — a centralizing imperial cult choice with parallels in Akhenaten's Aten, Aurelian's Sol Invictus, and the Japanese imperial Amaterasu.

The Sapa Inca was treated as a literal living deity: carried on a litter, eating from dishes ritually burned after each use, with hair clippings and nail trimmings collected and ritually disposed of because any part of his body had supernatural significance.

The Sapa Inca was supposed to marry his full sister Coya to keep the divine bloodline pure — the brother-sister marriage pattern echoes Pharaonic Egypt and the Hellenistic Ptolemies, and the standard interpretation is ideological rather than biological.

When a Sapa Inca died his mummified body was kept in a palace, dressed in royal robes, treated as if alive, and maintained by a panaca lineage of his descendants — and crucially did not pass his property to his successor, which forced each new emperor to conquer fresh territory to fund his court.

On festival days the mummies of past sapa incas were carried in procession to the central plaza of Cuzco, propped up on golden thrones, fed chicha, spoken to, and given a vote in dynastic decisions through their panaca representatives.

Capacocha was the Inca state ritual of child sacrifice — children selected for physical perfection, processed through Cuzco ceremonies, then walked to mountain summits where they were given chicha, fed coca, and either strangled or left to freeze.

Frozen capacocha mummies recovered from Llullaillaco at 22,109 feet, from Mount Ampato, from Cerro El Plomo, and from Aconcagua are in essentially perfect condition because of the freezing temperatures at the burial sites; their hair samples show elevated coca and alcohol consumption in the months before death.

Cuzco's sacred geography was organized into 41 ceque lines radiating from the Coricancha, connecting around 328 wak'a shrines — close to the number of days in twelve sidereal lunar months, suggesting the system also functioned as a ritual calendar.
subtitle: Inti, the Sapa Inca as living god, and the panaca cult of the royal mummies

title: Architecture and the Stone Mystery
bullets: Inca polygonal masonry fits irregular many-sided blocks together so precisely that no mortar is needed and a knife blade cannot be inserted between them — across thousands of individually cut stones in a single wall.

The largest Sacsayhuaman blocks weigh roughly 200 tons — at the upper end of pre-modern stone-moving scale and comparable to the heaviest Egyptian pyramid blocks, achieved without iron tools, draft animals other than the llama, or wheels.

Every door, window, and wall niche in Inca architecture has a trapezoidal shape narrower at the top — structurally more stable in earthquakes than rectangular openings and uniquely Inca, recognizable on sight wherever it appears in the Andes.

Inca foundation walls have survived every major earthquake in five centuries; the Spanish baroque churches built on top of them have had to be repaired or rebuilt several times after each major shock.

Vince Lee's experimental archaeology in the 1980s and 1990s reconstructed Inca stone-moving using the actual tools available — large teams of laborers, log rollers, packed-earth ramps, rope teams — and showed the techniques are well-attested in pre-modern stone-moving traditions across multiple cultures.

Moray's concentric circular terraces sunk into a natural depression have a temperature differential of up to 27°F between the top and bottom rings — most archaeologists read it as an experimental agricultural station for adapting crops to different elevation zones.

Ollantaytambo's Sun Temple is built from six pink granite blocks weighing 50 to 90 tons each, quarried six miles away across a river and a thousand feet lower in elevation — a logistical project that took thousands of mit'a laborers months for each individual stone.

The 'mystery' of Inca stonework is in important respects a function of how thoroughly the conquest destroyed the institutional preconditions: the mit'a is gone, the hereditary stonework guilds are gone, and what remains is stones that the post-conquest world cannot replicate.
subtitle: mortarless polygonal masonry, the trapezoidal signature, and how 200-ton blocks were actually moved

title: Smallpox Before the Spanish
bullets: Smallpox raced ahead of the Spanish conquerors and reached the Inca empire through trade contacts no later than 1524 — eight years before Pizarro's force landed on the Peruvian coast.

The standard estimate is that the Andean smallpox epidemic of 1524 to 1527 killed between one-third and one-half of the empire's population — several million people in a few years, in a virgin-soil population with no inherited immunity.
Huayna Capac and his designated heir Ninan Cuyochi both died of smallpox at Tumipampa around 1527, leaving the empire with an open succession at the worst possible moment.

Two of Huayna Capac's surviving sons fought a brutal three-year civil war for the throne: Huascar in Cuzco backed by the southern legitimist nobility, and Atahualpa in the north backed by his father's experienced Caranqui-war army.

Atahualpa's general Quizquiz defeated Huascar's army at the Battle of Quipaipan near Cuzco in early 1532, captured Huascar, and entered the imperial capital — just months before Pizarro arrived on the coast.

The post-civil-war purges decapitated the Cuzco nobility — Huascar's supporting panaca lineages were systematically executed, his mummy-cult institutions ritually desecrated — leaving the empire substantially weaker in 1532 than it had been in 1525.

The standard popular myth that 168 Spaniards conquered an empire of twelve million through audacity and steel understates the conditions: smallpox and civil war did the structural damage, and the Spanish exploited the resulting power vacuum.

The same combination — Old World disease arriving ahead of conquest, political crisis erupting under demographic stress, then a small invading force exploiting the resulting weakness — also took down the Aztec Empire twelve years earlier, on two continents to two small Spanish forces commanded by men who had read each other's reports.
subtitle: the epidemic that killed Huayna Capac and the civil war that gutted the empire

title: Cajamarca and After
bullets: Francisco Pizarro landed at Tumbes in May 1532 with 168 men — 62 mounted, 106 infantry — a military force smaller than a typical small-town American high school class, and marched into the Andes to confront the new emperor.

On 16 November 1532, Pizarro's 168 conquistadors ambushed Atahualpa in the central plaza of Cajamarca, killed several thousand unarmed Inca attendants in perhaps an hour, captured the emperor, and lost zero of their own.

Atahualpa offered to fill a 22-by-17-foot room once with gold and twice over with silver in exchange for his release — between 13,000 and 24,000 pounds of gold and 26,000 to 50,000 pounds of silver, the largest ransom in recorded human history.

The ransom was paid; Pizarro had Atahualpa publicly strangled by garrotte on 26 July 1533 anyway — the entire portable gold and silver of the Coricancha and the imperial treasury was melted into ingots for shipment to Spain.

Manco Inca was installed as a Spanish puppet Sapa Inca in late 1533, escaped supervision in early 1536, and besieged the few hundred Spaniards in Cuzco for ten months with an army of perhaps 100,000 mit'a-mobilized warriors — the structural weakness of mit'a mobilization caught up with the rebellion at planting season.

After the siege failed, Manco retreated into the cloud forest northwest of the Sacred Valley and founded the neo-Inca state at Vilcabamba, which lasted thirty-six years under Manco and his sons Sayri Tupac, Titu Cusi Yupanqui, and finally Tupac Amaru.

Tupac Amaru I was captured at Vilcabamba and publicly beheaded in the central plaza of Cuzco on 24 September 1572 by order of Viceroy Francisco de Toledo, ending the independent Inca state forty years after Cajamarca.

Tupac Amaru II's revolt in 1780 to 1781 mobilized perhaps 100,000 indigenous fighters across southern Peru and Bolivia, ended with his quartering in the same Cuzco plaza, and made the name Tupac Amaru a charged political symbol invoked by movements from Peruvian guerrillas to the American hip-hop artist Tupac Shakur.

Modern Peru and Bolivia built national identities on Inca symbolism — Quechua is a co-official language of perhaps 8 to 10 million speakers, Pachamama is venerated alongside the Virgin Mary across the rural Andes, and Machu Picchu is the country's primary international cultural symbol.
subtitle: Pizarro's 168, the ambush, the ransom, the execution, and the long aftermath
